persona.name: Karen Yoshida

occupation.title: Physical Therapist (DPT)
occupation.organization: Yoshida Physical Therapy & Sport Rehab (private practice)
occupation.description: Owns and operates a small PT clinic specializing in running injuries and post-surgical rehab. Sees 8-10 patients daily. Spends time on her feet demonstrating exercises, performing manual therapy, and educating patients about biomechanics.

persona.education: B.S. in Biology from University of Oregon. Doctor of Physical Therapy from OHSU. Board-certified Orthopedic Clinical Specialist (OCS). Additional certification in running gait analysis.
long_term_goals: Run pain-free for the rest of her life (realistic, not ambitious)
Grow her practice to include a second therapist
Publish a patient-facing guide to running injury prevention
Complete a half marathon again — her last was before ACL surgery
Mentor a new DPT graduate each year through her clinic
persona.style: Karen communicates with clinical precision softened by genuine warmth. She explains shoe biomechanics to patients daily, so her vocabulary is accessible but technical when needed. She speaks carefully — choosing words the way she chooses treatment plans: evidence-based, personalized, cautious of overclaiming. She dresses professionally in the clinic but switches to running clothes immediately after. Her tone is measured, empathetic, and slightly cautious — she's seen too many patients hurt by bad shoe choices to be casual about recommendations.

traits: Evidence-based in all decisions — clinical training shapes everything
Empathetic with patients and genuinely invested in their outcomes
Cautious about her own body — her ACL reconstruction taught humility
Quietly authoritative — doesn't need to raise her voice to be heard
Skeptical of shoe marketing that claims injury prevention without evidence
Values long-term joint health over short-term performance

interests: Physical therapy research and clinical practice
Hiking in the Columbia River Gorge
Reading medical journals on running biomechanics
likes: Shoes with moderate stability features that don't overcorrect
Brands that collaborate with biomechanics researchers
Products with transparent drop and stack height specifications
Neutral cushioning that allows natural foot mechanics
Running shoe stores with trained fitters
dislikes: Marketing that promises shoes will 'prevent injuries'
Maximalist cushioning trends that weaken foot intrinsics
Products positioned as replacements for proper rehab
Carbon plate shoes for casual runners who don't need propulsion
Brands that ignore pronation as a concept entirely
Shoes that change drastically between model years
Patients who buy shoes based on social media instead of getting fitted

beliefs: Shoes are one variable in a complex biomechanical system — they matter, but they're not magic
The best shoe is the one that complements your gait without masking underlying dysfunction
Injury prevention comes from training load management, not shoe technology
Evidence should drive shoe selection, not marketing spend
skills: Running gait analysis (2D video and force plate)
Orthopedic physical therapy and manual techniques

general: Jogs 3x per week — easy pace, never pushing since ACL surgery
Evaluates every shoe she wears through a clinical biomechanics lens
Recommends specific shoes to patients based on their gait analysis
Attends running medicine conferences annually (ACSM, ASPT)

evening: Cook dinner: often rice, fish, vegetables
Tea ceremony practice once a week
weekend: Saturday: longer jog (5-6 miles) on Wildwood Trail
Pottery class at a local studio
Sunday: hike with friends or errands

health.physical: ACL reconstruction (left knee) 4 years ago — fully rehabbed but she doesn't trust it for speed work or uneven terrain. Right hip occasionally tight from compensating post-surgery. Manages her training load carefully to avoid flare-ups. Strong core and upper body from years of demonstrating exercises.
health.mental: Grounded and self-aware. Accepts that her competitive running days are behind her. Finds fulfillment in helping patients return to running. Occasionally frustrated by patients who ignore her advice. Pottery and tea ceremony provide meditative balance.

relationships.name: Ken Yoshida
relationships.description: Architect. Not a runner but hikes regularly. They bonded over shared Japanese-American heritage and a love of Portland's food scene.

relationships.name: Mia Yoshida
relationships.description: Senior at UW studying public health. Calls weekly. Karen worries she doesn't exercise enough.

relationships.name: Dr. James Whitmore
relationships.description: Sends post-surgical patients to Karen's clinic. They discuss shoe recommendations over professional lunches.

relationships.name: Yoko Yoshida
relationships.description: Lives in Lake Oswego. Born in Japan. Karen visits every Sunday for tea and conversation in Japanese.
